narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, LSAT score of 18, Year 1 school decile of 3, Year 3 decile of 1, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 68% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your undergraduate GPA, each pulling the prediction in specific directions compared to typical students. Most pivotal was your Year 3 decile of 1, which is well below the average of about 5.5 and strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, signaling to it that your later academic performance ranked in the bottom tier of your peers. Your LSAT score of 18, far lower than the typical 37, was the second biggest driver, reinforcing the risk of failure by highlighting a key weakness in standardized legal reasoning skills. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, right around the average of 3.21, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, showing solid prior college performance. Overall, these stood out against patterns in the dataset, where stronger Year 3 rankings and higher LSATs are common among those who pass the bar. Your full-time status aligns with most students at 93%, so it didn't sway things much, nor did your middle income bracket. While this outcome is disappointing, especially given your background, it reflects data-driven patterns rather than a judgment on your potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above average, strengthening your Year 3 performance through additional coursework or prep, and reapplying to schools with more flexible criteria—many students in similar situations succeed by targeting these improvements. With focused effort, you can shift the odds in your favor for future applications.
